title: Enumerating Smooth-Like Permutations Via the Geometry of Schubert Varieties
speaker: Edward Richmond
abstract: In 1984, Ryan showed that any smooth Schubert variety in type A is an iterated fiber-bundle of Grassmannian varieties. Later, Haiman calculated the generating function for the number of smooth permutations (equivalently smooth Schubert varieties) of size n using this fiber-bundle structure. In this talk, I will discuss how several other families of "smooth-like" permutations can be enumerated using similar methods. Each of these families can be characterized by pattern avoidance similar to smooth permutations. This talk includes joint work with W. Slofstra and F. Azam.
tags: Connections to Schubert Calculus Learning Seminar

proof_stages.label: Type-A Schubert varieties and the smoothness-counting problem
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes the complete type-A flag variety, identifies torus fixed points with permutations, defines Schubert varieties X_w and their length/Bruhat-order structure, and poses the guiding questions of which X_w are smooth and how many such permutations there are.

proof_stages.label: Benchmark equivalences for smooth Schubert varieties
proof_stages.summary: Smooth Schubert varieties are presented through parallel characterizations: Lakshmibai-Sandhya avoidance of 3412 and 4231, rank-symmetric Bruhat intervals/rational smoothness, Ryan’s iterated Grassmannian fiber-bundle structure, and Haiman-style generating functions with exponential rather than factorial growth.

proof_stages.label: Smooth subfamilies: toric and polished permutations
proof_stages.summary: Two stricter smooth families are introduced: toric Schubert varieties, equivalent to Coxeter-element permutations, Boolean Bruhat intervals, and iterated P1-bundles; and polished permutations, equivalent to self-dual Bruhat intervals with additional length-five pattern avoidance and a later fiber-bundle/enumeration interpretation.

proof_stages.label: Mildly singular adjacent families from Schubert-type fibers
proof_stages.summary: The speaker then enlarges the smooth class by allowing iterated parabolic fiber bundles whose fibers are Grassmannian Schubert varieties, or only Grassmannians and Grassmannian Schubert divisors, giving corresponding pattern-avoidance lists and noting which generating functions are known or still unresolved.

proof_stages.label: Staircase diagrams and the smooth-permutation bijection
proof_stages.summary: Staircase diagrams are defined as partially ordered shifted intervals with controlled overlaps and gaps; a recursive maximal-block construction λ(D) using Levi/parabolic longest coset representatives gives a bijection with smooth permutations and recovers the Ryan-type fiber-bundle structure.

proof_stages.label: Enumeration and recognition in the diagram calculus
proof_stages.summary: Counting smooth permutations is reduced to counting staircase diagrams via Catalan/Dyck-path structure, while toric diagrams become singleton-block diagrams, polished diagrams become one-overlap zigzags, and labeled staircase diagrams explain the weighted enumeration and difficulty of the mildly singular superfamilies.

examples.name: Relative-order 3412 pattern
examples.note: The speaker explains containment of the pattern 3412 as four entries whose relative sizes are third-largest, largest, smallest, and second-smallest, making the pattern-avoidance criterion concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: he unpacks the 3412 pattern by determining the relative ranks of its four entries in a containing subsequence

passes.reason: ordinary length-four permutation pattern used as a forbidden obstruction to smoothness.

An ordinary length-4 permutation pattern, used as a forbidden obstruction whose occurrence makes the Schubert variety non-smooth.
3412 is an ordinary length-four permutation pattern used as a forbidden obstruction to smoothness.
3412 is an ordinary length-four permutation pattern used as a forbidden obstruction to smoothness.

retaining only V4 collapses a complete flag to the less-structured single-step Grassmannian projection.

examples.name: Distinct-simple-reflection reduced words
examples.note: Words such as 123 or 235 are used to illustrate Coxeter-element permutations whose reduced expressions involve no repeated simple reflection in the toric subclass.
examples.engagement_reason: he reasons through reduced words with distinct simple reflections, explaining their subwords give subsets with no cancellations and hence Boolean/Bott-Samelson behavior

passes.reason: no-repetition reduced words are the simplified Boolean/Coxeter-element case used to characterize the toric subfamily.
Reduced words with distinct simple reflections form a systematic Coxeter-element/Boolean-interval family, used as a positive characterization of toric Schubert varieties.
reduced words with distinct simple reflections have minimal Coxeter interaction and no repeated generators
Squarefree reduced words use the minimum possible length for their support and give Boolean subword/Bruhat structure, serving as a positive toric characterization.
Pairwise distinct simple reflections give Boolean Bruhat intervals, a maximally regular cube-like structure, but are used as a positive toric characterization.
square-free reduced words have no repeated simple reflections and hence a simplified Boolean subword structure.
reduced words with distinct simple reflections give Boolean Bruhat intervals, a maximally regular extremal subword situation characterizing the toric subclass.
Reduced words with distinct simple reflections are the low-complexity square-free case with Boolean Bruhat intervals, an extreme toric subclass rather than a counterexample.
a reduced word with distinct simple reflections has a Boolean Bruhat interval, the maximal-subword/maximally regular case
Reduced words with distinct simple reflections have length equal to support size and give minimal-complexity Boolean Bruhat intervals.
reduced words with distinct simple reflections are squarefree/Coxeter-type elements of minimal word complexity with Boolean Bruhat intervals.
Distinct simple reflections give Boolean Bruhat intervals, a maximally regular structure used to characterize the toric subclass.

examples.name: Type-C inverse Schubert varieties
